text: I've been cheering at the wrong moment during a rocket launch, because liftoff's just the start. A big test is about to happen, about a minute in, right here, at a point called Max Q. You can hear them say this in a launch, and how some of that works here. Here, the rocket's going really fast, but the air is still thick enough to push back hard, and that combo slams the rocket with the strongest aerodynamic force of the entire flight. The rocket typically throttles down to survive it. Listen for that. And then throttles back up once through Max Q. Then they're still waiting, because it still has to drop its used up fuel tank. Ignite its second set of engines, and fire them at just the right speed direction and altitude, so that the spacecraft orbits around Earth rather than falling back down to it. So next launch, I'm going to cheer with the engineers. And if you believe there should be more optimistic science and tech stories, follow along.
